stem: How many real solutions does \( [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]x + [[c]] = 0 \) have?
solution: The discriminant is \( [[b]]^{2} - 4([[a]])([[c]]) > 0 \), so the quadratic has two distinct real roots.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes a positive discriminant gives no real roots.
This wrongly assumes a positive discriminant gives a repeated (double) root.
A quadratic equation cannot have three real roots.
A quadratic equation cannot have four real roots.

stem: The quadratic \( [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]x + [[c]] \) is a perfect square times \( [[a]] \) (that is, \( [[a]](x-[[r]])^{2} \)). How many real solutions does \( [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]x + [[c]] = 0 \) have?
solution: The discriminant is \( [[b]]^{2} - 4([[a]])([[c]]) = 0 \), so the quadratic has exactly one real solution (a repeated root, at \( x=[[r]] \)).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes a zero discriminant gives no real solutions.
This double-counts the repeated root as two distinct solutions.
A quadratic equation cannot have three real roots.
A quadratic equation cannot have four real roots.

stem: The parabola \( y = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]x + [[c]] \) never crosses the \( x \)-axis. How many real solutions does \( [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]x + [[c]] = 0 \) have?
solution: The discriminant is \( [[b]]^{2} - 4([[a]])([[c]]) < 0 \), so the quadratic has no real solutions (its two roots are complex conjugates).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes a negative discriminant still gives one real (repeated) root.
This wrongly assumes a negative discriminant still gives two real roots.
A quadratic equation cannot have three real roots.
A quadratic equation cannot have four real roots.

stem: How many real solutions does \( |[[a]]x + [[b]]| = [[c]] \) have?
solution: Since \( [[c]] > 0 \), the equation splits into \( [[a]]x+[[b]]=[[c]] \) and \( [[a]]x+[[b]]=-[[c]] \), each with one solution, giving two distinct real solutions in total.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes a positive right-hand side gives no real solutions.
This keeps only one of the two branches of the absolute value equation.
This overcounts, as if a third branch existed.
This overcounts, as if a fourth branch existed.

stem: The right-hand side of \( |[[a]]x + [[b]]| = [[c]] \) is negative. How many real solutions does this equation have?
solution: An absolute value is never negative, so \( |[[a]]x+[[b]]| = [[c]] \) (with \( [[c]] < 0 \)) has no real solutions.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly treats the negative right-hand side as if it still gave one solution.
This wrongly assumes the two usual absolute-value branches both still apply here.
This overcounts an already-impossible equation.
This overcounts an already-impossible equation.

stem: How many real solutions does the absolute-value equation \( |[[a]]x + [[b]]| = 0 \) have?
solution: \( |[[a]]x+[[b]]| = 0 \) exactly when \( [[a]]x+[[b]]=0 \), which has exactly one real solution, \( x=-\dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes an absolute value equal to zero has no solution.
This wrongly double-counts as if the two usual absolute-value branches gave distinct solutions here (they coincide).
This overcounts, as if a third branch existed.
This overcounts, as if a fourth branch existed.

stem: How many real solutions does \( [[a]]x^{3} + [[B]]x^{2} + [[C]]x + [[D]] = 0 \) have?
solution: This cubic factors as \( [[a]](x-[[r1]])(x-[[r2]])(x-[[r3]]) = 0 \), giving three distinct real solutions: \( x=[[r1]], [[r2]], [[r3]] \).
distractor_rationales: Every cubic with real coefficients has at least one real root, so the count cannot be zero.
This misses two of the three real roots.
This misses one of the three real roots.
A cubic equation has at most three roots in total, so it cannot have four real solutions.

stem: How many real solutions does \( [[a]]x^{3} + [[B]]x^{2} + [[C]]x = [[negD]] \) have?
solution: Rearranged, this is \( [[a]]x^{3} + [[B]]x^{2} + [[C]]x + [[D]] = 0 \), which factors as \( [[a]](x-[[r1]])(x^{2}+[[cc]]) = 0 \). Since \( x^{2}+[[cc]]=0 \) has no real solution ( \( [[cc]] > 0 \) ), the only real solution is \( x=[[r1]] \).
distractor_rationales: Every cubic with real coefficients has at least one real root, so the count cannot be zero.
This wrongly treats the irreducible quadratic factor as contributing two real roots.
A cubic equation has at most three real roots, but the quadratic factor here contributes none.
A cubic equation has at most three roots in total, so it cannot have four real solutions.

stem: How many real solutions does \( [[a]]x^{4} + [[B]]x^{3} + [[C]]x^{2} + [[D]]x + [[E]] = 0 \) have?
solution: This quartic factors as \( [[a]](x-[[r1]])(x-[[r2]])(x^{2}+[[cc]]) = 0 \). Since \( x^{2}+[[cc]]=0 \) has no real solution, the real solutions are just \( x=[[r1]] \) and \( x=[[r2]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the irreducible quadratic factor removes ALL real solutions.
This keeps only one of the two genuine real solutions.
This wrongly treats the irreducible quadratic factor as contributing two real roots on top of the other two.
A quartic has at most four roots in total, but the quadratic factor here contributes none of them.

stem: How many real solutions does \( e^{2x} + [[coefB]]e^{x} + [[coefC]] = 0 \) have?
solution: Substituting \( u=e^{x} \) gives \( u^{2} + [[coefB]]u + [[coefC]] = (u-[[u1]])(u-[[u2]])=0 \), so \( u=[[u1]] \) or \( u=[[u2]] \). Both are positive, so both give a real \( x=\ln u \), for two real solutions in total.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes neither root of the substituted quadratic is achievable by \( e^{x} \).
This keeps only one of the two valid positive roots.
This overcounts, as if the substituted quadratic had a third root.
This overcounts, as if the substituted quadratic had a fourth root.

stem: How many real solutions does \( e^{2x} + [[coefB]]e^{x} = [[negCoefC]] \) have?
solution: Rearranged, this is \( e^{2x} + [[coefB]]e^{x} + [[coefC]] = 0 \). Substituting \( u=e^{x} \) gives \( u^{2} + [[coefB]]u + [[coefC]] = (u-[[u1]])(u-[[u2]])=0 \), so \( u=[[u1]] \) or \( u=[[u2]] \). Since \( u=e^{x} \) must be positive, only \( u=[[u1]] \) is achievable, giving exactly one real solution, \( x=\ln([[u1]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes even the one valid positive root is not achievable by \( e^{x} \).
This wrongly counts the negative-or-zero root as also giving a valid real \( x \).
This overcounts, as if the substituted quadratic had a third root.
This overcounts, as if the substituted quadratic had a fourth root.

stem: How many real solutions does \( \sin(x) = \dfrac{[[num]]}{8} \) have on \( 0 \le x < 2\pi \)?
solution: Since \( -1 < \frac{[[num]]}{8} < 1 \), the horizontal line \( y=\frac{[[num]]}{8} \) crosses one full period of \( y=\sin(x) \) exactly twice on \( [0,2\pi) \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the right-hand side is outside the range of \( \sin(x) \).
This applies the count for the special cases \( \sin(x)=\pm 1 \), which do not apply here.
This overcounts, as if a third crossing occurred in one period.
This overcounts, as if a fourth crossing occurred in one period.

stem: How many real solutions does \( [[base]]^{x} = [[rhs]] \) have?
solution: \( [[base]]^{x} \) is strictly increasing over all real \( x \) and takes every positive value exactly once, so \( [[base]]^{x}=[[rhs]] \) (with \( [[rhs]] > 0 \)) has exactly one real solution, \( x=\log_{[[base]]}([[rhs]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes a positive right-hand side is unreachable.
This wrongly assumes an exponential equation behaves like a quadratic with two solutions.
This overcounts, as if the equation could have three solutions.
This overcounts, as if the equation could have four solutions.
